speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number eight, and you were just leaving the camp to go to the station to board the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now it was the 31st of July, a lovely day, and the two camps-- sister camps-- we all met, about 600 of us, young, strong people. Mostly of us-- most of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was still with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And we met there and sort of we-- I remember vividly that I usually tried to go into a corner or against the wall. I was one of the first in the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can-- were you in the same wagon as your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, next to me. Oh, yes. We were always together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe what the wagon was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- whatever it was, a goods train, but an open-- no roof. You know, an open wagon which, this particular open and closed situation, will be of great importance at the slightly later stage. But at this, it was open. It was summer. You know, nice weather. We did not fear. We, you know, hoped we would go into another camp. The Russians were coming near, so it's one step nearer the end. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Opposite me was a very nice lady who I've never seen before-- young lady-- and was rather tall. She was sitting opposite me. Our glances met, but I looked away shyly, because at that time I was rather shy. And, well, I just-- that crossed my mind. Something nice to look at during the journey. The train was going rather slowly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] direction Germany, away from the Russians, of course. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the mood was quite good, generally. We didn't know where we were going. We were just hoping.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any water or food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we took-- yes. In the middle of the-- in the middle of the wagon, we-- we had our luggage with us. In the middle of the wagon was a bucket with jam, of which a bit-- of which more a bit later. Now the mood, as I said, was not too bad. After about-- I'm not sure-- an hour, between an hour or two, going very slowly, the train had to stop. Couldn't see a station, but somebody stood on some luggage, looked across the other wall, looked outside, and he must have seen somebody, you know, a uniformed man in, you know, with perhaps the insignia of a-- you know somebody working in a nearby station, at a railway. And he sort of-- sort of gestured where we would go, for instance. You know, couldn't shout, because we didn't know if he had guards. We didn't see any guards. We didn't know if-- if we had and if something good happened, I'm not sure. But anyway, from the distance, the man made-- gave this sign, we were told. Now obviously, when we were-- when he stepped off his luggage and told us, our mood immediately changed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then my father and I, you know, we had sort of like a discussion. Vielleicht it was only [INAUDIBLE]. Vielleicht-- maybe. I'm speaking German. Vielleicht. Maybe it was only on the [INAUDIBLE]. Maybe-- you know, maybe he knows something. Train's going to [INAUDIBLE]. Who knows what. Never heard of again. But he doesn't know. But probably he knew more than we. Anyway, so the mood was bad, and in the evening we reached-- it was a very slow train-- we reached a town called Gliwice. Now I sort of make a couple of remarks which I have not made in my book because I only learned about it later. When Hitler wanted to start the war, he sent some soldiers, I think in Polish uniform. I mean, he starteed an incident in that particular town. That's a border town. Under-- under which, you know, which gave him the pretext to start the war, to march to Po-- into Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, that was particular-- I didn't know it at the time, but Gliwice, I said to my father. Gliwice? You know, it's a big town. Have you heard anything? Well, we don't know of any camp or anything, but maybe there's a camp-- another camp here, a sister camp. And then suddenly you heard a voice. One of the passengers said, if the engine-- it was evening-- stays at the front, we'll go-- it's a good sign. We'll go to a camp to Germany. If he goes to the back, it'll go to Auschwitz. The engine. Now I have-- the Auschwitz was not anything special in my mind. I mean, probably I've heard of it, but the moment-- the reaction of the others, who knows what it was-- I suddenly had a terrible feeling. I was a bundle-- a bundle of nerves in no time, and the nerves-- by a nervous reaction, I had sort of a-- I had a terrible need to go somewhere, and what would you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now it was night, and like a conjurer who wants to hide what other people shouldn't see, I managed to take sort of a tea towel out of our baggage, put it on my little place underneath me and I did what I hoped other people didn't see, and the bundle went overboard. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It belongs to the story, and that's what it was. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you on the train for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I continue this story. Now, the lights in front of us were all red, and I have a whole chapter of this particular incident, 'A Night to Remember,' I called it. And I prayed for the lights to be green and let us go. And my prayers grew in fervor. Eventually they actually became green, and we were pulled, again, forward. And we were going near the green, under the green, past the green. Thank you, God, for saving us. And then a little while later the cra-- train stopped, just outside the station. A line of red again. And despondency, again. And I stared at the light, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the engine was uncovered. Panic again. The engine drew almost forward, and then alongside us, and then stopped. And suddenly I heard gushing water and I said, oh my. What an idiot I am to be thrown from despair into hope, from hope to despair in, you know, such rapid succession. And, you know, it's ridiculous. Of course the steam locomotive, in those days, needed water. The water stopped and I said, all right. Now, get back to the front and get us out of here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The water stopped and all I heard is steps, moving away. The driver had left us as well. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And before he left, I s-- I almost willed-- I saw the contours of this engine-- willed that powerful beast to move and go, go and do its job, but in the front. But as I say, the driver moved away and it-- that quiet, the night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And suddenly, I stepped on some luggage and looked out and I saw only-- you know, my-- my-- my eyes probably were-- were trained onto the red lights too much. And I couldn't see the ground. I always thought I was surrounded by water. I had the feeling, but obviously we were not. But it was so quiet that I said, this is the time to escape. And like a few times before, the big question which must have happened thousands and thousands of times over, which thwarted the idea of escape, was the question of where to. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The countries of Europe were occupied, and maybe the engine will go to the front. And I wanted to think of something to make a rope, to step outside, because where were the guards, I thought. Step outside in case somebody would come, I would climb back quickly. But we had no ropes to put a coup-- put a couple of sheets together.

speaker: interviewer
text: But once an escape has been started, there's no coming back. Anyway, had we known what-- but nobody else did. So where are the so-called toughies and go-getters amongst the youngsters? Why did they-- well, maybe some did, but we didn't hear anything. We didn't see anything. So we waited as well. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Now my nerves were really bad at that time, and I fell into a kind of stupor. Maybe half sleep or whatev-- whatever you'd like to call it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was woken up by a jerk. The engine was back. You know, coupled to the train, but at the rear. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the train started moving and soon veered to the right. And once more it stopped, and when it stopped, what I didn't see before, every second train had a guard. The nearest to us was on the next wagon the other side, so not very near. And I were sitting on some ledges on top of the, you know, of the wagons. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the train gathered speed. Whereas it was going abnormally slow the day before, it gathered such a speed, it went so fast, that I said to my father, this speed is mean-- meant to make it impossible to jump. We know there's no more-- no more doubt now. We have to escape, come what may. And I said, to confuse the guards, I jump on one side, you on the other. We do the same time. I step on the head of the shoulder of the person opposite, and you on the head of the sho-- shoulder and head of this one to reach the wall and then we jump at the same time. And to do that, I count two, and on the-- on your count of three, we'll jump. Come with me, because Auschwitz is the end. So my father said, OK. But before we do that, he would take out every scrap of socks or what have you to put around your hand. And everything else, dress to make the fall sort of easier. The impact. And that's what we did. And this day-- glorious summer's day, first of August-- we were looking like we were going to the North Pole. And the neighbor onto whose head my father was to step, got up, eyed us conspic-- suspiciously, and said, I was-- oh, by the way, I was counting one, two, I was ready for the tiger jump. But the three never came because the neighbor had stood up. My father also eyed the area and hoped for a patchy a-- you know, grass or something, you know. A patch of grass, you know, to-- to ease the fall. And then a neighbor said-- anyway, before he said the three-- if you don't [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sit down over here, or if you don't stop, I'll call the guards.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was like-- like a-- I don't know. You know, to be in such tension, and then, of course, you have to go back to normal. And to him I said, what does it matter? We are dead anyway. And whatever he mumbled, I didn't understand anymore. But the moment, it passed, and I sat down. And this moment-- at this moment, I almost felt relief, because what would have happened outside? If one of us would have been hurt, the train probably wouldn't have stopped because, to be shot by the guards would-- you know, it's-- it's also unlikely because we were two sides on-- and-- and this speed, I don't think the bullet would have hit us. But if we were hurt, what should the other one do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, it also-- I also thought-- but that was of course at a much later date-- this moment of relief is the syndrome, what I call the live another minute syndrome. How many people, for the reason of this syndrome, live another minute, have missed the opportunity of escaping, or going away, or running away in the last minute, most of which probably would have ended in death anyway, and people knew it. But it would have been the death I was always thinking about. If I should be killed, it should-- should be by-- with a bullet in the back. Anyway [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the moment of relief that I haven't jumped was short. Soon over. Soon it came back, the full weight of the thought of this is going to be the end. Auschwitz. Although I didn't know exactly by what method. I think some people mentioned the word gad and some people, you know. But it was not quite clear because whenever I heard of another camp, I was only concerned with the one at present-- my life for the day, for tomorrow maybe-- but not with another camp. We always-- it always came pretty sudden when we were put from one camp to another and never being told how and where and when.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, long story short, that in no time, the train slowed down. And to where? In front of Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I saw some people throwing items into the bucket, and I wondered, what the hell is that? Are they thinking to retrieve it later? On the other hand, if not, who is enrich-- is going to enrich themselves with that? And why do this right away? It seemed peculiar. And-- What sort of things did they throw in the bucket?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know, but what can they throw? Money, coins, rings. Who knows what? I'm not sure why or what made them do that. You see, I was only seeing it. I was wondering.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, of course, the full weight came on-- you know-- to, me say now, how long from now? And I put a blanket over my head and really, sort of, life started passing by in my mind. And I thought, why should I be killed? What have I or my father, who actually fought the fir-- the First World War in German uniform? Why? Why have I been saved all the time mehr-- more with a mir-- a miracle than by, sort of like, by any design? Why should I be killed now?

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the midst of it all, my father's voice came to me and my ever so practical father said, should we be gassed, breathe deeply. Breathe deeply and get it over with. And as I say, don't fight it. Shorten the ordeal. And that I have taken as the title to my book, only I had a writer's license. I added 'My son.' So the book is called, in memory of this moment-- and of my father's advice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what could I answer? I didn't answer, and I didn't know what to answer. I-- And I still was under the blanket. And I suddenly had a feeling as if something was hovering above me. A distinct feeling. I don't know what it was. I called it a phenomenon, my guardian angel maybe.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had it in a vague sort of manner later on in life, and I think-- still think that I have such a guardian angel apart from any religion. And I took off the blanket and I [INAUDIBLE] suddenly felt ready, come what may. Probably in the last minute I will run and get the bullet in the back and that will be it. I'm not going to go into any gas chambers. But the second picture was, but if I should only be wounded. But I stopped at that thought. I didn't want to think anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, already-- I already mentioned the-- the syndrome, the live another minute syndrome, where-- which thwarted the attempt of thousands of other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why, people always asked afterwards. Why did people go to the slaughter like lambs? And I put it to this particular live another minute syndrome. Something may happen in that minute, you know. And it-- So when you sat in-- with the blanket over your head, you were sitting still on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the train, yes. Next--

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was still moving, or it was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was standing. Next moment, we heard the shouting. Raus. Schnell, schnell. You know, the usual shout.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first impression as you got off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, ready for danger, but not knowing what to do, of course. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went out and we were segregated. Now, I didn't know about the other segregations, where old was parted from young. In our case it was just parted women from men, and at that particular moment I was glad I didn't have mother and sister with me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were, in no time, marching, ha-- having still our luggage and marching slowly. Nobody actually pushed us hard too much faster. In the distance, I saw some buildings. It was a long road. And before that, even before we left the train, I saw a group of marching people, uniformly dressed in these famous now pajama-like suits. And I said to my father at the time-- it was before we left-- oh, people are marching here, probably to work. Maybe there's a chance. We are a young group. And again, like it was all the time, hopes, panic, hopes, panic.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we went along the way, I saw another chap working on the side of the road, and I ask him, tell me-- in passing-- is this our last? And I didn't know how to end the sentence. And as I already finished the sentence, I was only a couple of steps away, and I turned round. He didn't look at me, but he bent his head like in a nod.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, you can imagine the rest of the way. Anyway, we were in-- we-- we-- we sort of were put into some barracks again. And people are still take off their--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was bef-- did you go straight to the barracks, or did you have to go--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. On the end of the road there were barracks. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't have to go and get your uniforms or anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. No. That's going to come. And I saw people, again, re-arranging their valuables, possible, and doing this, and you know. And I-- and I said to him, what are you doing? That's the end. You know. He turned around with the back to me so I shouldn't see what he's doing and-- anyway. Next step, we were again, led from the barracks into a-- what I would now call a sort of anteroom, ante-washroom, ante-- where we had to undress. Now [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at this moment, other inmates started shaving us. All body hair and head, of course. And I said to my father, they don't need to do that if they're going to kill us. And behind us, a group of young women came in laughing to-- to themselves. Not taking any notice of us, the scene of us, you know, a-- a-- a row of naked men meant nothing to them. And they had to take their luggage-- our belongings-- away and probably trying to look through and-- or handing in the valuables. I'm not sure how much has disappeared and hopeful for the future. I don't know if there is still millions, maybe, to be buried there. I don't know. Anyway, these were the so-called Canada commandos, written about and spoke about quite often, which were every three months killed anyway. But that's-- I'm-- I'm veering away from the subject. And that is also why I wrote the book, because any time I wanted to sp-- speak, I could not stick to a subject, always veering away into another vision. Oh, I have to tell you that. I have to tell you that. And then I couldn't find my way back. But since I had written the book, that's how I can-- that's why I can tell, more or less, the story straight, really only a little bit. Anyway, we left everything except the one postcard which I had for my mother, with a scribble-- with a message in the back. And I tore the edges to a minimum, so I can still have the writing and the picture, and to fit into my palm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I went through the door, somebody said, what have you got there? Well, it was only-- you could see a picture. An inmate, and he smiled-- smiled politely, nicely. Only later I wondered what this man must have seen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we went into the gas chambers, but not knowing it-- on-- hoping it wasn't. You know, we were, of course, like everybody else, told that's the shower room, the bathroom, shower room. So heavy doors closed behind us. I looked up and I saw shower hoses. And we had water. When the water came, hope-- life and hope came back to me. And even only very young people can do that. I kept to myself what I really wanted to say. Hello, daddy. Complain. The water's not very warm. But that's sort of a show-- a show of it, you know. But we had water. And then we were led out at the opposite side. A door opened-- not where we had left our belongings-- and there we received our pajama-like suits, and a hat as well. Now I'm not-- I can't even remem-- remember what shoes we had. I can't remember if we had any shirts. But that's about what I do remember. I do remember stepping out into the sunshine at around 11:00, I would say. And ever since, I make sure that 11:00, on always the fir-- first, I look out and say hello to the-- to the life-giving sun, which meant life, in gigantic letters.
